welcome.slide1Desc: SpineFlow shows which exercises are suitable for YOUR specific curve type and surgery history — always listen to your body.
welcome.slide2Desc: Every exercise is rated Guided, Modify, or Avoid — general fitness inspiration personalized to your profile.
welcome.slide3Desc: Log workouts, track volume, hit personal records — always listening to your body.

consent.title: Before we begin
consent.subtitle: Please read and accept the following documents to continue using SpineFlow.
consent.tosContent:
By using SpineFlow, you agree to the following terms:

1. SpineFlow is a fitness companion app for informational purposes only.
2. You must be at least 16 years old to use this service.
3. Your account may be terminated for violations of these terms.
4. Subscription payments are handled by Apple/Google and are subject to their terms.
5. SpineFlow reserves the right to update these terms with notice.
6. These terms are governed by the laws of Switzerland.

Full terms available at www.spineflow.app
consent.tosAccept: I accept the Terms of Service
consent.privacyContent:
SpineFlow collects and processes the following data:

• Email address (for authentication)
• Scoliosis type and surgery history (for personalized safety ratings)
• Workout logs (for progress tracking)
• Nutrition logs (for calorie tracking)

Your health data is stored securely in Switzerland (Zurich) and is never shared with third parties. You can delete all your data at any time via Settings.

SpineFlow complies with the GDPR (EU) and nDSG (Swiss Federal Data Protection Act).

Full policy available at www.spineflow.app
consent.disclaimerContent:
IMPORTANT — PLEASE READ CAREFULLY

SpineFlow provides general fitness education, not medical treatment. SpineFlow is NOT a medical device and does NOT provide medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment.

ALL EXERCISES ARE PERFORMED AT YOUR OWN RISK. SpineFlow accepts NO LIABILITY for any injury, damage, or harm arising from the use of this application.

The guidance ratings ("Guided", "Modify", "Avoid") are GENERAL FITNESS INSPIRATION ONLY. They are not personalized medical recommendations.

You MUST consult a qualified healthcare professional before beginning any exercise program, especially if you have had spinal surgery.

If there is any conflict between SpineFlow's suggestions and your doctor's instructions, ALWAYS FOLLOW YOUR DOCTOR.

Full disclaimer available at www.spineflow.app
consent.disclaimerAccept: I accept the Medical & Liability Disclaimer

experience.beginnerDesc: 0–6 months · Fewer exercises, 3 sets each — focus on learning form
experience.intermediateDesc: 6–24 months · Balanced volume with moderate exercise count
experience.advancedDesc: 2+ years · More exercises per muscle group, up to 5 sets

onboarding.customDisclaimer: Custom equipment has no guidance ratings — proceed with care and listen to your body.

home.safetyDesc: All exercises have guidance ratings for your profile. This is general fitness education — always consult a healthcare professional and listen to your body.
home.chartInfoBody:
Training volume per day = sum of weight × repetitions across all completed sets (in kg).

Bodyweight exercises are not included in the volume.

Higher values = more work done.

performance30d.title: Training experience?
performance30d.subtitle: This determines exercise selection and intensity.
performance30d.beginnerDesc: 0–6 months · Fewer exercises, 3 sets each — focus on learning form
performance30d.intermediateDesc: 6–24 months · Balanced volume with moderate exercise count
performance30d.advancedDesc: 2+ years · More exercises per muscle group, up to 5 sets

exerciseDetail.avoidWarning: This exercise is not recommended for your curve type. Performing it may cause injury. Consult your doctor before attempting.

exerciseTips.1: Each arm moves independently — perfect for correcting the muscle imbalances scoliosis creates.
exerciseTips.2: One of the best exercises for scoliosis! Builds symmetrical back strength. Shoulder-width grip.
exerciseTips.3: The front-loaded weight forces an upright torso — much safer for your spine than back squats.
exerciseTips.4: One of the best exercises for scoliosis! Directly strengthens postural muscles.
exerciseTips.5: Spine is fully supported. Safer than squats. Focus on pushing equally with both legs.
exerciseTips.6: The backrest stabilizes your spine. Dumbbells allow natural movement.
exerciseTips.7: Isolation exercise — no spinal load. Still brace your core and don't use momentum.
exerciseTips.8: The trap bar centers load around your body, reducing spinal shear.
exerciseTips.9: Corrects the forward shoulder posture common in scoliosis. Essential.
exerciseTips.10: Unilateral work is gold for scoliosis! Train the weaker side first.
exerciseTips.11: Guided path with back support — great for beginners with scoliosis.
exerciseTips.12: Perfect warm-up exercise. Activates the postural muscles you need for every other exercise.
exerciseTips.13: The incline reduces lower back stress compared to flat bench. Builds the upper chest for a balanced look.
exerciseTips.14: Constant cable tension is great for scoliosis — smooth movement, no jerking, easy to control imbalances.
exerciseTips.15: Free, anywhere, anytime. Engages the entire anterior chain. Use a mirror to check for asymmetric arm push.
exerciseTips.16: Isolation for quads with zero spinal load. Use single-leg to check for left-right strength differences.
exerciseTips.17: Hamstrings support the pelvis and lower back. Strengthening them helps reduce back pain from scoliosis.
exerciseTips.18: Use dumbbells (not barbell). Keep back neutral at all times. Reduce range of motion if needed. Use mirror.
exerciseTips.19: One of the best glute exercises with minimal spinal compression. Upper back is supported on the bench.
exerciseTips.20: Seated version removes all lower body momentum — pure shoulder isolation. Use mirror to catch uneven lifting.
exerciseTips.21: Machine-supported rear delt work — perfect for scoliosis. The chest pad keeps your spine stable.
exerciseTips.22: The bench support takes load off your spine. Great for finding and fixing left-right imbalances.
exerciseTips.23: The chest pad stabilizes your torso — you can focus purely on your upper back. Great for posture correction.
exerciseTips.24: Builds the brachialis for thicker arms. Neutral grip is easier on the wrists. Zero spinal load.
exerciseTips.25: Standing cable work with zero spinal load. Keep elbows locked at your sides — let the triceps do all the work.
exerciseTips.26: The overhead position stretches the long head of the tricep for maximum growth. Cable version is safer than dumbbell.
exerciseTips.27: Cable provides constant tension (unlike dumbbells which lose tension at the top). Single-arm option great for imbalance work.
exerciseTips.28: The #1 core exercise for scoliosis. Trains your body to resist rotation — directly counters the rotational forces of scoliosis.
exerciseTips.29: Controlled rotation strengthens the obliques and teaches rotational control. But use with caution — rotation can be risky for some curves.
exerciseTips.30: Seated position = zero spinal load. Can do with dumbbells on knees or on a calf machine.
exerciseTips.31: Excellent for fixing leg-to-leg imbalances common in scoliosis. The balance challenge also trains your core.
exerciseTips.32: Lying on your back = zero spinal load. Teaches your deep core muscles to fire. Do this before every workout as activation.
exerciseTips.33: Classic rehab exercise used by physios worldwide. Trains anti-extension and anti-rotation simultaneously.
exerciseTips.34: Isometric hold = no spinal movement. Strengthens the entire core at once. But proper alignment is critical with scoliosis.
exerciseTips.35: Targets the lats without heavy spinal loading. The cable keeps constant tension throughout.
exerciseTips.36: Single-arm cable work lets you match the effort side-to-side — ideal for correcting shoulder asymmetry from scoliosis.

exerciseMods.1: Use dumbbells instead of barbell for independent arm movement. No excessive arching.
exerciseMods.3: Hold dumbbell at chest. Use mirror to check symmetry. Avoid barbell back squats.
exerciseMods.6: Always seated with back support. Use dumbbells. Don't overextend overhead.
exerciseMods.8: ONLY with trap bar. Avoid conventional deadlifts! Reduced weight, perfect form.
exerciseMods.13: Set bench to 30-45 degree incline. Avoid going too heavy — focus on controlled reps.
exerciseMods.15: Keep core tight to avoid sagging. Use mirror to check if shoulders are level. Start on knees if needed.
exerciseMods.18: Use dumbbells (not barbell). Keep back neutral at all times. Reduce range of motion if needed. Use mirror.
exerciseMods.19: Keep chin tucked, ribs down. Don't hyperextend at the top. Use pad on barbell. Start with bodyweight.
exerciseMods.20: Seated to eliminate momentum. Light weight, controlled movement. Check mirror for even arm height.
exerciseMods.22: Support yourself with one hand on bench. Keep back flat and core braced. Don't rotate torso.
exerciseMods.26: Use cable version (not dumbbell) for constant tension. Keep core braced. Don't flare elbows.
exerciseMods.28: Stand sideways to cable/band. Press straight out from chest. Resist the rotation — that's the exercise.
exerciseMods.29: Use light weight. Control the rotation — don't let the cable pull you. Keep hips stable, rotate from the torso.
exerciseMods.31: Keep torso upright. Hold dumbbells at sides. Use wall or rack for balance if needed. Check mirror for lateral lean.
exerciseMods.34: For S-curve: focus on keeping hips level. Use mirror or ask someone to check alignment. Shorten hold time if form breaks.
exerciseMods.35: Keep core tight. Don't overextend at the top — stop when arms are parallel to floor.
exerciseMods.36: Stand sideways to cable. Keep slight bend in elbow. One arm at a time — great for imbalance correction.

exerciseFusionMods.1: Reduce weight by 30-50%. No arching at all — keep back flat. If upper fusion, use incline only.
exerciseFusionMods.2: Use lighter weight, strict form. Pull to chest only (never behind neck). Keep torso upright, no leaning back.
exerciseFusionMods.3: Only with doctor approval. Partial range of motion. Consider leg press as safer alternative.
exerciseFusionMods.4: Well-suited post-surgery. Keep torso stationary. Don't lean forward past 90 degrees. Light to moderate weight.
exerciseFusionMods.5: Good option post-surgery. Spine well-supported. Don't let knees come too close to chest. Start with very light weight.
exerciseFusionMods.6: Avoid if upper thoracic fusion. For lumbar fusion: seated with back support, partial ROM, light weight only.
exerciseFusionMods.7: Gentle option post-surgery. Seated is preferred over standing to reduce spinal sway. No momentum.
exerciseFusionMods.8: Avoid post-fusion. Spinal compression + hip hinge motion puts excessive stress on fused and adjacent segments.
exerciseFusionMods.9: Gentle option post-surgery. Light resistance. May help rebuild upper back and shoulder posture.
exerciseFusionMods.10: Good post-surgery. Keep torso completely still — no rotation. Light weight, focus on the squeeze.
exerciseFusionMods.11: Good post-surgery choice. Guided movement with back support. Start very light. Don't lock out fully.
exerciseFusionMods.12: Well-suited post-surgery. Low spinal load. Suitable for daily posture support. Start with light band.
exerciseFusionMods.13: Good option for upper fusion patients. Keep weight moderate, no arching.
exerciseFusionMods.14: Gentle option post-surgery. Keep weight light and focus on the squeeze. Standing or seated both fine.
exerciseFusionMods.15: Proceed with caution. Keep core rigid — no sagging. Wall push-ups or incline push-ups are lower-load alternatives.
exerciseFusionMods.16: Well-suited post-surgery. Spine supported by seat. Low spinal load.
exerciseFusionMods.17: Gentle option post-surgery. Lying position supports spine. Avoid seated leg curl if lumbar fusion.
exerciseFusionMods.18: Avoid post-fusion. Hip hinge movement puts direct stress on lumbar spine. Use leg curl machine instead.
exerciseFusionMods.19: Proceed with caution for lumbar fusion. Bodyweight or light only. Don't hyperextend.
exerciseFusionMods.20: Gentle option post-surgery. Seated reduces spinal sway. Keep weight very light.
exerciseFusionMods.21: Good post-surgery. Machine provides stability. Chest pad supports torso. Keep weight light.
exerciseFusionMods.22: Proceed with caution. Use very light weight. Keep torso completely rigid — no rotation at all.
exerciseFusionMods.23: Good post-surgery. Chest pad provides support. Keep weight light, focus on the squeeze.
exerciseFusionMods.24: Gentle option post-surgery. Seated preferred. No swinging or momentum.
exerciseFusionMods.25: Gentle option post-surgery. Keep elbows pinned to sides. No leaning forward. Use rope or bar attachment.
exerciseFusionMods.26: Caution with upper fusion — overhead position can stress thoracic segments. Use pushdowns as alternative.
exerciseFusionMods.27: Gentle option post-surgery. Constant tension throughout movement. Keep elbows stationary.
exerciseFusionMods.28: Well-suited post-surgery. Anti-rotation training may support spines with fusion. Keep weight very light.
exerciseFusionMods.29: Avoid post-fusion. Rotational movement directly stresses fused segments. Use Pallof Press as a lower-load alternative.
exerciseFusionMods.30: Gentle option post-surgery. Low spinal load in seated position.
exerciseFusionMods.31: Only for experienced lifters post-surgery. Requires good balance. Consider leg press single-leg as a lower-load alternative.
exerciseFusionMods.32: Well-suited post-surgery. Lying on back, low spinal compression. Suitable for rebuilding core activation.
exerciseFusionMods.33: Gentle option post-surgery. On hands and knees — low spinal load. Suitable for rebuilding back stability.
exerciseFusionMods.34: Proceed with caution. Keep hold times short (15-20s). Forearm plank is easier than full plank. Stop if any pain.
exerciseFusionMods.35: Only with light weight. Limit range of motion. Cable version is a lower-load option than dumbbell.
exerciseFusionMods.36: Gentle option post-surgery. Cable provides smooth resistance. Sit on a bench if standing is uncomfortable.

workout.modifyForm: Use modified form — see exercise details
workout.greatJob: Great job! Here is your summary:
workout.aiAnalysisDesc: Get your workout evaluated and compared with your last 5 sessions.

workout.aiDisclaimer: This app provides general fitness education, not medical treatment. This AI-generated analysis is for informational purposes only and does not replace advice from a qualified healthcare professional.

workout.cancelConfirmMsg: Your progress will be lost. Are you sure?
workout.highlightShowedUp: You showed up and put in the work!

progress.criticalHint: Critical for your scoliosis — needs more training
progress.noRecordsDesc: Complete workouts to see your personal records here

nutrition.bodyDataSubtitle: Needed for your personalised nutrition plan — entered once.
nutrition.metabolicHint: Needed solely for the scientific calculation of your basal metabolic rate.

settings.title: Settings
settings.reassessConfirm: This will regenerate your exercise recommendations based on your current profile (curve type, surgery, equipment). Continue?
settings.deleteAccountWarning: This will permanently delete your account and ALL associated data (workouts, nutrition logs, profile). This action cannot be undone.
settings.deleteAccountError: Failed to delete account. Please try again or contact support.

1.s1: Lie flat on bench, feet on floor

2.s2: Pull bar down to upper chest

6.s1: Sit with back supported, dumbbells at shoulders

8.s1: Stand inside trap bar, feet hip-width

9.s1: Face cable, set at head height
9.s3: Spread hands apart, squeeze rear delts

11.s1: Sit in machine, back against pad

12.s1: Hold band at chest height, arms out

14.s2: Open arms out to the sides

15.s4: Keep body in a straight line

16.s1: Sit in machine, feet under pad

17.s1: Lie face down, ankles under pad

18.s1: Stand tall, dumbbells in front of thighs
18.s2: Hinge forward at hips, slight knee bend

19.s1: Upper back on bench, feet flat
19.s4: Hold at top, full hip extension

20.s2: Raise arms out to shoulder height

21.s1: Sit facing machine, chest on pad

22.s1: One hand on bench for support

24.s1: Stand tall, neutral grip on dumbbells

25.s2: Push bar down, elbows at sides
25.s3: Lock out at bottom, squeeze triceps

27.s1: Stand facing low cable, grip handle

28.s2: Press hands straight out from chest

30.s1: Sit with weight on knees, balls of feet on edge
30.s3: Raise up on toes as high as possible

31.s3: Push through front heel, stand up

32.s1: Lie on back, arms and knees up
32.s2: Extend opposite arm and leg out

33.s2: Extend opposite arm and leg straight

34.s2: Keep body in a rigid line

35.s2: Pull cable down in a wide arc

36.s2: Raise arm out to shoulder height

offline.aiUnavailable: You are offline. AI analysis is available once you are back online. Your workout has been saved locally.
offline.aiWorkoutUnavailable: AI workout needs internet. You can use the local workout generator — it works offline.

ai.needMoreWorkouts: At least 2 workouts needed for analysis.
